# Standard deviation (σ)

FANGS parameterises the normal distribution using the **standard deviation** (σ, written `sigma` in models). The second argument of `dnorm` is the SD:

```
y[i] ~ dnorm(mu[i], sigma)
```

Here `sigma` is the residual standard deviation — the typical size of the gap between an observation and its fitted mean.

## Different from BUGS/JAGS/NIMBLE

This is a deliberate difference from the BUGS family. BUGS, JAGS and NIMBLE parameterise the normal by **precision** (τ = 1/σ²) instead:

| Software | `dnorm(mean, ?)` |
|----------|-----------------|
| FANGS | standard deviation σ |
| R `dnorm()` | standard deviation σ |
| Stan | standard deviation σ |
| BUGS / JAGS / NIMBLE | precision τ = 1/σ² |

FANGS uses the SD because it is the most directly interpretable scale for teaching: σ is in the same units as the response. If you are copying a model from JAGS/BUGS, remember to replace the precision argument with a standard deviation.

## The relationships

```
variance  σ² = sigma * sigma
precision τ  = 1 / (sigma * sigma)
```

A **small** σ means observations are tightly clustered around the mean; a **large** σ means they are spread out.

## Typical priors on σ

A simple, transparent choice is a uniform prior over a positive range:

```
sigma ~ dunif(0, 100)
```

This is weakly informative for most teaching datasets (the response is usually well within ±100 of its mean) and keeps σ positive. Because there is no conjugate update for the SD of a normal, FANGS samples `sigma` with the slice sampler — the mean parameters (`alpha`, `beta`, random effects) still receive exact conjugate updates.
